definition: Largest artery carrying oxygenated blood from the heart
rationale: The aorta is the main artery leaving the left ventricle distributing oxygenated blood to the entire body. It arches over the heart and descends through the thorax and abdomen branching to all organs.

definition: Large veins returning deoxygenated blood to the heart
rationale: The superior and inferior vena cava are the largest veins returning deoxygenated blood from the body to the right atrium. Superior drains the head and arms inferior drains the abdomen and legs.

definition: Blood vessels that supply the heart muscle itself
rationale: Coronary arteries branch from the aorta and supply oxygenated blood to the myocardium. Blockage causes myocardial ischemia and infarction. The left and right coronary arteries are the main branches.

definition: Major artery in the neck supplying the head and brain
rationale: The carotid arteries run along both sides of the neck supplying blood to the brain face and head. The carotid pulse is palpated in the neck and is used for pulse checks in adults.

definition: Major artery in the groin and thigh
rationale: The femoral artery is the major artery of the lower extremity running through the groin and thigh. The femoral pulse can be palpated in the groin crease. Femoral bleeding can be life-threatening.

definition: Artery at the wrist used for pulse assessment
rationale: The radial artery runs along the lateral or thumb side of the wrist. It is the most common site for pulse assessment in conscious patients. Absence of radial pulse may indicate shock.

definition: Artery in the upper arm used for blood pressure and infant pulse
rationale: The brachial artery runs along the inner upper arm. It is used for blood pressure measurement with a stethoscope placed over it. In infants it is the preferred pulse check site.

definition: Artery behind the inner ankle bone
rationale: The posterior tibial artery runs behind the medial malleolus inner ankle bone. Its pulse can be palpated there to assess lower extremity circulation especially after injury.

definition: Artery on top of the foot
rationale: The dorsalis pedis artery runs across the top of the foot. Its pulse can be palpated to assess foot circulation. Absence may indicate arterial disease or injury.

definition: Throat area behind the mouth and nose
rationale: The pharynx is the throat extending from the back of the nasal cavity to the larynx and esophagus. It is divided into nasopharynx oropharynx and laryngopharynx. It is part of both airway and digestive tract.

definition: Voice box containing the vocal cords
rationale: The larynx or voice box connects the pharynx to the trachea. It contains the vocal cords and epiglottis. The cricoid cartilage at its base is the only complete cartilage ring in the airway.

definition: Flap that covers the airway during swallowing
rationale: The epiglottis is a leaf-shaped cartilage that folds over the glottis during swallowing to prevent aspiration. Epiglottitis causes it to swell potentially obstructing the airway completely.

definition: Point where trachea divides into right and left bronchi
rationale: The carina is the ridge at the tracheal bifurcation where it divides into right and left main bronchi. It is located approximately at the level of the sternal angle or second intercostal space.

definition: Smallest airways leading to alveoli
rationale: Bronchioles are the smallest conducting airways less than 1mm diameter leading to the alveoli. They lack cartilage and are surrounded by smooth muscle that can constrict in asthma.

definition: Tiny air sacs where gas exchange occurs
rationale: Alveoli are microscopic air sacs at the end of bronchioles where oxygen and carbon dioxide exchange with blood occurs. There are approximately 300 million alveoli providing enormous surface area for gas exchange.

definition: Membrane surrounding the lungs
rationale: The pleura is a double-layered membrane. The visceral pleura covers the lungs and the parietal pleura lines the chest wall. The potential space between them normally contains only a thin fluid layer.

definition: Potential space between lung and chest wall membranes
rationale: The pleural space is the potential space between visceral and parietal pleura. Normally it contains minimal fluid. Air pneumothorax or blood hemothorax in this space compresses the lung.

definition: Primary muscle of breathing separating chest and abdomen
rationale: The diaphragm is the dome-shaped muscle separating thorax from abdomen. Contraction flattens it increasing thoracic volume and creating negative pressure for inhalation. It provides about 70% of breathing effort.

definition: Muscles between the ribs assisting breathing
rationale: Intercostal muscles are located between ribs. External intercostals elevate ribs during inspiration while internal intercostals depress ribs during forced expiration. They are accessory muscles of respiration.

definition: Largest part of brain controlling thought and voluntary movement
rationale: The cerebrum is the largest brain portion divided into left and right hemispheres. It controls higher functions including thought memory speech sensation and voluntary movement. Different lobes control different functions.

definition: Brain portion controlling coordination and balance
rationale: The cerebellum is located at the back of the brain below the cerebrum. It coordinates movement balance and posture. Damage causes ataxia uncoordinated movement and balance problems.

definition: Lower brain controlling vital functions like breathing
rationale: The brain stem connects the brain to the spinal cord and controls vital functions including consciousness breathing heart rate and blood pressure. It includes the midbrain pons and medulla.

definition: Brain stem portion controlling breathing and heart rate
rationale: The medulla oblongata is the lowest portion of the brain stem containing centers controlling respiration heart rate blood pressure and reflexes like swallowing and vomiting. Damage is usually fatal.

definition: Protective membranes covering the brain and spinal cord
rationale: The meninges are three protective membranes: dura mater outermost arachnoid mater middle and pia mater innermost. They protect the CNS and contain cerebrospinal fluid. Meningitis is their inflammation.

definition: Fluid surrounding and cushioning the brain and spinal cord
rationale: CSF fills the ventricles and subarachnoid space cushioning and protecting the brain and spinal cord. It also removes waste and provides nutrients. CSF leakage after trauma indicates skull fracture.

definition: Controls involuntary functions like heart rate and digestion
rationale: The autonomic nervous system controls involuntary functions without conscious thought. It has two divisions: sympathetic fight or flight and parasympathetic rest and digest with opposing effects.

definition: Fight or flight response system
rationale: The sympathetic nervous system prepares the body for stress causing increased heart rate bronchodilation pupil dilation and blood flow to muscles. Epinephrine mimics sympathetic activation.

definition: Rest and digest response system
rationale: The parasympathetic nervous system promotes rest and recovery causing decreased heart rate increased digestion pupil constriction and energy conservation. It opposes sympathetic effects.

definition: Breastbone in the center of the chest
rationale: The sternum is the flat bone in the center of the anterior chest. It connects to the clavicles and ribs. Chest compressions are performed on the lower half of the sternum.

definition: Collar bone connecting shoulder to sternum
rationale: The clavicle connects the sternum to the scapula and is commonly fractured from falls on outstretched arm or direct trauma. It can be palpated along its entire length.

definition: Shoulder blade on the posterior thorax
rationale: The scapula is the triangular bone on the posterior thorax forming part of the shoulder joint. It is protected by overlying muscles but can be fractured by significant trauma.

definition: Upper arm bone
rationale: The humerus is the single bone of the upper arm extending from shoulder to elbow. The radial nerve wraps around it and can be injured with humeral fractures.

definition: Lateral forearm bone on the thumb side
rationale: The radius is the lateral forearm bone on the thumb side. Its distal end at the wrist is commonly fractured in falls on outstretched hand Colles fracture. The radial pulse is palpated near it.

definition: Medial forearm bone on the pinky side
rationale: The ulna is the medial forearm bone on the pinky side. The olecranon process forms the point of the elbow. It articulates with the humerus at the elbow.

definition: Hip bones forming the lower trunk
rationale: The pelvis is formed by the two hip bones ilium ischium pubis sacrum and coccyx. It protects pelvic organs supports the spine and transfers weight to legs. Pelvic fractures cause major bleeding.

definition: Thigh bone the largest bone in the body
rationale: The femur is the largest strongest bone extending from hip to knee. Femur fractures require significant force and can cause 1-2 liters of blood loss into the thigh.

definition: Kneecap
rationale: The patella is the sesamoid bone embedded in the quadriceps tendon that protects the front of the knee joint. It can be fractured by direct trauma or forceful muscle contraction.

definition: Shin bone the larger bone of the lower leg
rationale: The tibia is the larger medial bone of the lower leg bearing most of the body weight. Its anterior surface the shin is subcutaneous and commonly injured. It articulates with femur and talus.

definition: Smaller lateral bone of the lower leg
rationale: The fibula is the smaller lateral bone of the lower leg. It does not bear significant weight but provides muscle attachment and forms part of the ankle joint. It commonly fractures with ankle injuries.

definition: Seven neck vertebrae
rationale: The seven cervical vertebrae C1-C7 form the neck portion of the spine. They are smaller and more mobile than other vertebrae. Cervical spine injury can cause paralysis or death.

definition: Twelve chest vertebrae articulating with ribs
rationale: The twelve thoracic vertebrae T1-T12 form the upper and mid back. Each articulates with a pair of ribs. They are less mobile than cervical vertebrae due to rib attachments.

definition: Five lower back vertebrae
rationale: The five lumbar vertebrae L1-L5 form the lower back. They are the largest vertebrae bearing most of the body weight. Low back pain often originates from this region.

definition: Membrane lining the abdominal cavity
rationale: The peritoneum lines the abdominal cavity and covers most abdominal organs. Peritonitis is inflammation of this membrane usually from infection or organ rupture causing severe abdominal rigidity and pain.

definition: Located behind the peritoneum
rationale: Retroperitoneal structures are located behind the peritoneal cavity including kidneys ureters aorta vena cava and pancreas. Bleeding here may not cause peritoneal signs initially.

definition: Largest solid organ in the abdomen located in right upper quadrant
rationale: The liver is located primarily in the RUQ under the right ribs. It is highly vascular and liver lacerations cause significant hemorrhage. It metabolizes drugs produces bile and stores glycogen.

definition: Blood-filtering organ in the left upper quadrant
rationale: The spleen is located in the LUQ under the left ribs. It filters blood and stores red blood cells. It is the most commonly injured organ in blunt abdominal trauma and bleeds profusely when ruptured.

definition: Organ producing insulin and digestive enzymes
rationale: The pancreas is located behind the stomach in the retroperitoneum. It produces insulin for glucose regulation and digestive enzymes. Pancreatitis causes severe abdominal pain.

definition: Organs filtering blood and producing urine
rationale: The kidneys are retroperitoneal organs filtering blood to produce urine and regulate electrolytes fluid balance and blood pressure. Kidney injury can cause significant bleeding and is often from flank trauma.

definition: Small pouch attached to the cecum in the right lower quadrant
rationale: The appendix is a small blind pouch attached to the cecum in the RLQ. Appendicitis is inflammation causing RLQ pain that classically migrates from the umbilicus. Rupture causes peritonitis.

definition: Outermost layer of skin
rationale: The epidermis is the outermost skin layer providing a barrier against pathogens and water loss. It contains no blood vessels and is constantly shed and regenerated from below.
